mnemonics.title: PAVE: Pointers Are Very Important for Everything
mnemonics.content: P-A-V-E stands for: Pointers store Address, Variables store data, Everything in memory has an address, Reference is an alias. This reminds students that pointers store addresses, not actual values.

mnemonics.title: Star Seeks Value, Ampersand Seeks Address
mnemonics.content: The asterisk (*) operator dereferences (gets the value at the address), while ampersand (&) gets the address of a variable. Think: * says 'show me what's inside', & says 'where is it located'.

mnemonics.title: DECLARE: Declare Explicitly Correct Address Reference Every Time
mnemonics.content: D-Declare, E-Explicitly, C-Correct, A-Address, R-Reference, E-Every, T-Time - reminds students to always initialize pointers and declare reference variables properly.

mnemonics.title: Reference = Real Alias Not Copy
mnemonics.content: R-Refers to original, A-Alias (another name), N-Not a new variable, C-Changes affect original, N-Not a separate memory location, N-Null not allowed.

acronyms.usage: Use before a variable name to get its memory address. Example: &x gives address of x. Essential for initializing pointers.
Use in declaration (*ptr) to indicate pointer type, or before pointer variable to access value it points to. Example: *ptr accesses the value at the address stored in ptr.
Common naming convention: int* ptr or int *ptr. Use 'ptr' suffix or prefix to identify pointer variables in code.

acronyms.expansion: No Useful Address Link / Not Under Any Local Linkage
acronyms.usage: Initialize pointers to NULL when not pointing to valid memory. Always check if (ptr != NULL) before dereferencing to avoid segmentation faults.

acronyms.expansion: Dereferenced Address No Longer Giving Logical Value - Reference Gone
acronyms.usage: A pointer pointing to freed memory or invalid address. Occurs when memory is deallocated but pointer still references it. Avoid by setting pointer to NULL after delete.
C++ specific: int& ref = var creates reference 'ref' as alias for 'var'. No & operator needed when using reference (unlike pointers).

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box labeled 'x' with value '25' inside. Draw an arrow from a variable 'ptr' pointing to box 'x'. Write 'ptr = 0x1000' (address of x) inside the ptr box. The pointer IS the arrow holding the address.
Picture following the arrow: ptr holds address 0x1000 → follow arrow → reach box x → show value 25. The * operator is the action of following the arrow to retrieve what's inside.
Draw one box labeled 'age = 30'. Place a second tag next to it reading 'age (nickname: years)'. Both names point to SAME box. Changes to 'years' instantly change 'age'. No second box created.
Box 'ptr1' holds address 0x1000. Box 'ptr2' holds address of ptr1 (0x2000). Draw nested arrows: ptr2 → ptr1 → actual data box. **ptr2 gets value by following two arrows.
Draw a pointer box with text 'NULL' or value 0 (or nullptr). Show a broken/faded arrow with an 'X' - pointing to nothing. This indicates the pointer is not ready to use.
Two rows: Row 1 - One box with two name tags (reference - no new memory). Row 2 - One box with arrow pointing from separate pointer box (pointer - separate memory for address).

analogies.analogy: A pointer is like a written address on a piece of paper. The paper (pointer variable) is not the house (actual data), but it tells you WHERE the house is located. To visit the house, you follow the address - that's dereferencing.

A reference variable is like giving a nickname to your best friend. Whether you call them 'John' or 'Johnny', you're referring to the SAME person. If Johnny gets a haircut, John also has a new haircut - changes affect the original directly.

If you have an array of houses on a street (consecutive memory), incrementing a pointer is like walking to the NEXT house. ptr + 1 moves to the next integer in memory - you move by one 'house' (sizeof(int)) bytes.

The pointer is your mailbox address. The value inside the mailbox is what you get when you open it. *ptr is like opening the mailbox to retrieve the letter (value). Without a valid address (NULL/wild pointer), you can't get mail.

Before using a GPS, you must enter a destination address. Similarly, always initialize pointers: int* ptr = &variable; or int* ptr = nullptr; An uninitialized pointer is like a GPS with no destination - it will take you nowhere predictable.

analogies.concept: Dynamic Memory Allocation as Renting Space
analogies.analogy: Using 'new' to allocate memory is like renting a storage unit. You request space, get a unit number (address returned), and can store items there. Using 'delete' is like returning the unit - the address becomes invalid but the pointer still holds the old unit number (dangling pointer).
